reviews.summary: This paper presents a carefully controlled empirical study investigating whether rich early-training trajectory features improve pseudo-group inference for spurious correlation mitigation compared to simpler output-only statistics under matched computational budgets. The main finding is negative: trajectory features slightly improve validation clustering fidelity at T=4 (NMI 0.321 vs 0.311) but do not translate to better worst-group accuracy (0.491 vs 0.535), with the pattern also failing to transfer to soft-reweighted ERM.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I conducted 5+ distinct searches: (1) paper title - not found, confirming this is original work; (2) SPARE (Yang et al. 2024) - a related method that uses early outputs for importance sampling, but differs in focusing on reweighting rather than matched-budget signal comparison; (3) "matched-budget trajectory pseudo-group" - no exact matches found; (4) Correct-N-Contrast (Zhang et al. 2022) - uses contrastive learning but from ERM predictions, not early trajectories; (5) ExMap (Chakraborty et al. 2024) and Han & Zou (2024) - related group inference methods but without the matched-budget trajectory comparison. The novelty lies in the careful controlled comparison design rather than a new algorithm. The negative finding that higher pseudo-group fidelity doesn't improve downstream robustness is a meaningful contribution, though limited by single-dataset evaluation.
strengths: Rigorous experimental design with matched budgets isolates the specific question of signal quality vs compute cost
All numerical claims in the paper are verified against results.json - perfect integrity
Well-executed ablations (no shape code, uniform weights, transfer to soft-rwERM) strengthen conclusions
Clear distinction between pseudo-group fidelity metrics and downstream worst-group accuracy - an important conceptual contribution
Honest presentation of negative results with appropriate caveats about scope limitations
Complete artifact with runnable code, detailed logs, and reproducible experimental pipeline
weaknesses: Limited to a single real dataset (Waterbirds) for main results - Colored MNIST is synthetic and single-seed
Claimed transfer test to soft-rwERM also shows trajectory underperforms (0.483 vs 0.545 WG), contradicting any general 'fidelity helps' hypothesis
Warmup budgets limited to only T=2 and T=4 epochs - very short trajectories with limited signal diversity
No comparison to stronger baselines like SPARE (which achieves 91.6% WG on Waterbirds)
The uniform weights ablation performs better than the full method (0.511 vs 0.491 WG at T=4), suggesting the weighted contrastive formulation itself may be problematic
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper presents a careful controlled study with appropriate caveats. To strengthen for acceptance: (1) Expand dataset coverage - add CelebA or UrbanCars to verify the negative result generalizes; (2) Consider longer warmup budgets (T=8 or T=10) to test if trajectory benefits emerge with richer signal; (3) Include direct comparison to SPARE or Correct-N-Contrast as stronger baselines; (4) Address why uniform weights outperform weighted SupCon - this is an interesting finding that deserves analysis; (5) The Discussion section correctly notes the BIC-selected K sensitivity check was skipped - consider at least a minimal evaluation; (6) The paper would benefit from clearer articulation of why the fidelity-robustness gap exists - is it due to downstream optimization issues or fundamental limits of early trajectory signal?
questions_for_authors: Why does the uniform weights ablation (which ignores pseudo-group quality) achieve better worst-group accuracy than the full trajectory method? Does this suggest the problem is in how pseudo-groups are used rather than their quality?
Did you explore any trajectory lengths beyond T=4? The extremely short warmup may not provide enough signal diversity for trajectory features to show their value.

The soft-rwERM transfer test also shows trajectory underperforms output-only. How do you interpret this - is the pseudo-group inference simply not informative enough, or is the issue with the downstream learners?
Can you compare your results directly to SPARE or Correct-N-Contrast which achieve much higher worst-group accuracy on Waterbirds?
reviews.integrity_check: Verified complete integrity: (1) All numbers in Table 1 and text match results.json exactly (e.g., Trajectory T=4 WG 0.491173±0.067613 matches raw run 0.425/0.584/0.464); (2) Experiment code in exp/shared/core.py correctly implements trajectory encoding, xg-SupCon, and soft-rwERM; (3) Logs show actual training runs with expected epoch progression; (4) All cited references verified real - Arefin et al. 2024, Chakraborty et al. 2024, Han & Zou 2024, Yang et al. 2024 all exist with correct titles and venues; (5) Code is runnable and generates figures/tables from actual results.

reviews.summary: The paper studies a narrow but relevant question for annotation-free group robustness: whether short early-training trajectories provide better pseudo-group signals than matched-budget output-only statistics or warmup-end features. Using Waterbirds as the main benchmark and Colored MNIST as a sanity check, it finds a mostly negative result: trajectory features can slightly improve pseudo-group fidelity at T=4, but this does not translate into better worst-group accuracy under either contrastive or reweighted ERM downstream training.
reviews.novelty_assessment: I performed the required novelty checks, including searches for the exact title on Google Scholar/Semantic Scholar (no exact scholarly match found), searches for the core idea ('early training trajectories' / 'pseudo-group inference' / 'spurious correlations'), searches around cited baselines and their neighbors, and searches over recent NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR papers on annotation-free group robustness. The closest prior work I found is SPARE, which uses early outputs to identify spurious bias early in training (https://arxiv.org/abs/2305.18761); Beyond Distribution Shift studies spurious features via training dynamics but as analysis rather than pseudo-group inference (https://arxiv.org/abs/2302.09344); GIC argues that better pseudo-group inference is the bottleneck (https://proceedings.mlr.press/v235/han24g.html); ExMap clusters explainability heatmaps for unsupervised group robustness (https://openaccess.thecvf.com/content/CVPR2024/papers/Chakraborty_ExMap_Leveraging_Explainability_Heatmaps_for_Unsupervised_Group_Robustness_to_Spurious_CVPR_2024_paper.pdf); DISK infers spurious/domain labels automatically (https://openreview.net/forum?id=XwAT4LSaXe); and Self-Guided Spurious Correlation Mitigation constructs fine-grained pseudo-labels without annotations (https://www.ijcai.org/proceedings/2024/619). I did not find an existing paper with the same matched-budget trajectory-vs-output-only benchmarking question, so the paper is not a duplicate. However, the contribution is still incremental because it is primarily a controlled comparison built on ideas already present in SPARE-style early-signal methods and broader pseudo-group inference literature, rather than a substantively new method.
strengths: The paper asks a clear, well-scoped question and answers it directly with a controlled matched-budget comparison rather than overclaiming algorithmic novelty.

The negative result is potentially useful: the experiments suggest that modest gains in pseudo-group fidelity do not necessarily improve worst-group accuracy, which is a meaningful caution for this literature.

The empirical setup is more careful than many workshop-style studies: warmup budgets are matched, downstream learners are controlled within comparisons, and the paper includes both transfer to soft reweighted ERM and component ablations.
Artifact quality is relatively strong overall. Source code is present, results are aggregated into machine-readable summaries, and the paper’s table values match the saved summaries/results.
The paper is clearly written, contributions are explicit, and limitations are stated rather than hidden.
weaknesses: The scientific contribution is modest for NeurIPS. The paper is mainly a benchmark-style negative result on one primary dataset, not a new method or a broad empirical study.

Novelty is limited by substantial overlap in ingredients with existing work on early spurious-bias detection, training dynamics, and annotation-free pseudo-group inference; the main new element is the particular controlled comparison.
Experimental scope is narrow: Waterbirds is the only real multi-seed benchmark, Colored MNIST is only a single-seed sanity check, and the optional K-sensitivity study was not run.

Statistical support is weak for some claims. Only three seeds are used, variance is large in several conditions (especially T=2), and there are no significance tests or confidence intervals beyond standard deviations.

The artifact hygiene is imperfect: several vanilla-SupCon CSV logs contain duplicated epoch blocks, suggesting repeated appends or reruns without log cleanup. This does not appear to invalidate the main reported numbers, but it weakens confidence in the logging pipeline.

Pseudo-group fidelity is evaluated with oracle validation groups, but its relationship to downstream robustness is central to the paper’s claim; the paper establishes correlation failure in this setup, yet does not offer a deeper analysis of why the fidelity metrics fail as predictors.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper would benefit from being framed more explicitly as a benchmark/analysis paper rather than as a method paper. To strengthen the submission, I would first expand the empirical scope: add at least one more natural benchmark beyond Waterbirds, run multiple seeds for Colored MNIST or replace it with a more informative secondary dataset, and complete the missing K-sensitivity analysis since the two-cluster assumption is central to the pseudo-group pipeline. Second, strengthen the statistical analysis: report confidence intervals or hypothesis tests for the matched-budget gaps, especially where seed variance is large, and discuss effect sizes rather than only means. Third, deepen the analysis of the fidelity/robustness disconnect: for example, study whether certain classes, groups, or posterior-calibration properties of the GMM assignments are driving the failure to transfer from better NMI to better worst-group accuracy. Finally, clean up the released artifacts so each run has a single unambiguous warmup/finetune log; the current duplicated epoch blocks in some logs are not fatal, but they are below the standard expected for a polished NeurIPS submission.
questions_for_authors: Can you add at least one additional real benchmark to test whether the negative matched-budget result is specific to Waterbirds?
Why was model selection done by validation average accuracy rather than an annotation-free robustness proxy, and how sensitive are conclusions to that choice?
Do the conclusions survive alternative numbers of inferred groups or a model-selection rule over K?
Can you explain the duplicated epoch blocks in some vanilla-SupCon logs and confirm whether they came from reruns/appends rather than from any issue in checkpoint selection or result aggregation?
Do you have any analysis showing which fidelity metric, if any, is most predictive of worst-group accuracy across seeds and methods?
reviews.integrity_check: The integrity check is mostly positive. Experiment source code is present and implements the described warmup -> pseudo-group inference -> downstream training pipeline; the paper’s reported table numbers match the saved aggregates/results; and the figures are generated from saved tables/results by code rather than appearing hand-fabricated. Per-run logs show plausible epoch-by-epoch training behavior. The main concern is artifact hygiene rather than fabrication: some `vanilla_supcon` CSV logs contain duplicated epoch blocks, indicating log appending or reruns without cleanup. Because the core paper numbers still match `results.json`/`results_summary.json`, I do not view this as evidence of fabricated results, but it does lower confidence somewhat.

reviews.summary: This paper presents a controlled empirical study asking whether short early-training trajectory features (confidence, margin, loss, augmentation disagreement, feature drift) provide better pseudo-group signal than output-only statistics or warmup-end features for annotation-free group robustness. The main finding is negative: on Waterbirds, trajectory features slightly improve validation pseudo-group fidelity (NMI) at T=4 warmup but do not improve worst-group accuracy under either cross-group supervised contrastive learning or soft pseudo-group reweighted ERM. A Colored MNIST sanity check (single seed) corroborates that fidelity gains don't translate to robustness gains.
reviews.novelty_assessment: Online searches for the exact paper title, for 'pseudo-group inference training dynamics spurious correlation', for 'matched budget comparison early training signal group robustness', and for recent NeurIPS/ICML/ICLR 2024-2025 proceedings did not reveal an existing paper performing this exact matched-budget comparison. The closest prior work is SPARE (Yang et al., AISTATS 2024) which uses early outputs for importance sampling, and GIC (Han & Zou, ICML 2024) which argues group inference quality is the bottleneck. However, the contribution here is essentially a negative ablation study over signal types for pseudo-group inference using well-known components (GMM clustering, SupCon loss, standard trajectory statistics). The trajectory-shape encoding (6 summary statistics per scalar sequence) is trivial and does not constitute a methodological novelty. The paper honestly acknowledges this framing, but the combination of known ingredients tested on a narrow scope limits novelty.
strengths: Honest and transparent reporting of a negative result — the paper does not overclaim and clearly states the hypothesis was refuted
Well-controlled experimental design: matched warmup compute budgets, fixed downstream learners, and testing across two downstream objectives (xg-SupCon and soft-rwERM)
Excellent reproducibility: full experiment code in exp/shared/core.py, per-run results.json files, warmup and finetune logs with training curves, config files, and checkpoints for every run
Clear and well-organized writing with explicit contribution statements, consistent notation, and self-contained tables with descriptive captions
Per-seed results transparently reported in the appendix, making the high-variance pattern visible to readers
The observation that pseudo-group fidelity can decouple from downstream worst-group accuracy is a useful cautionary note for the community
weaknesses: Extremely narrow experimental scope: only one real-image benchmark (Waterbirds) with 3 seeds, plus a single-seed Colored MNIST sanity check. This is insufficient to draw confident conclusions about when trajectory signals help or hurt.

High variance undermines the main claims: at T=4, output-only WG is 0.535±0.055 and trajectory WG is 0.491±0.068 — these confidence intervals overlap substantially, making it impossible to claim a statistically significant difference with 3 seeds.

No statistical significance tests are reported despite the paper making comparative claims ('trajectory is worse'). With 3 seeds and overlapping standard deviations, the ordering could easily reverse with additional seeds.

The planned BIC-selected-K sensitivity analysis was acknowledged as skipped (SKIPPED.md in ablation folder), yet the fixed K=2 assumption is a strong restriction that may interact with signal quality in ways the paper cannot assess.

No direct comparison against existing pseudo-group inference methods (SPARE, GIC, ExMap) — the paper compares signal families but not against the actual methods that use those signals in practice, limiting the practical value of the findings.

The contribution reduces to 'we tried adding augmentation disagreement and feature drift to the clustering signal and it didn't help on Waterbirds' — this is a single negative data point, not a systematic understanding of when trajectory signals succeed or fail.

Colored MNIST results actually show trajectory is better on fidelity but ERM beats both methods on WG — this suggests the downstream learner may be the bottleneck rather than the signal, which the paper does not adequately explore.
reviews.detailed_feedback: The paper is well-written and honest, which is commendable. However, for a venue like NeurIPS, the contribution needs to be substantially broader. The core issue is that a negative result on one dataset with 3 seeds and overlapping confidence intervals is not a robust finding — it is a preliminary observation. To strengthen this work: (1) Add at least 2-3 more datasets with real spurious correlations (CelebA, MultiNLI, MetaShift) to test whether the negative result generalizes. (2) Increase to 5-10 seeds and report statistical significance tests (paired t-tests or Wilcoxon signed-rank). (3) Complete the BIC K-sensitivity analysis — this is critical because the fixed K=2 assumption may disproportionately harm trajectory signals that detect finer-grained substructure. (4) Compare against SPARE and GIC as actual baselines, not just conceptual references, to show where trajectory-based pseudo-groups stand relative to existing methods. (5) Investigate why fidelity and WG decouple — the paper observes this but provides no analysis. Is it noise amplification? Miscalibrated cluster posteriors? The uniform-weights ablation improving WG suggests the weighting scheme is a confound, which deserves deeper investigation. (6) The Colored MNIST finding that ERM outperforms all pseudo-group methods deserves discussion — it may indicate that on datasets with very strong spurious correlations, the warmup model itself is too corrupted for any signal extraction to be useful.
questions_for_authors: Have you run paired significance tests on the 3-seed results? Given the overlapping standard deviations, can you confidently claim the trajectory signal is worse rather than merely noisier?

The uniform-weights ablation improves WG from 0.491 to 0.511 with the same trajectory pseudo-groups — does this suggest the main problem is how pseudo-groups are used (weighting scheme) rather than the signal quality itself?
Why was the BIC K-sensitivity check skipped? Could trajectory signals benefit from K>2 clusters if they detect finer subgroup structure than output-only signals?
On Colored MNIST, ERM beats both pseudo-group methods. Does this hold with multiple seeds? If so, what does it imply about the pipeline's utility on strongly correlated data?
Have you considered testing on CelebA or MultiNLI to verify the negative result generalizes beyond Waterbirds?
reviews.integrity_check: Results integrity is strong. I verified per-run results.json files (e.g., trajectory_xgsupcon_T4_seed7 WG=0.4252 matches appendix Table 5) and confirmed aggregate mean/std computations match paper tables. The experiment code in exp/shared/core.py correctly implements the described pipeline: trajectory_shape() computes the 6-element descriptor from Eq. 3, fit_signal_models() uses K=2 diagonal GMM with PCA to 16 dims when needed, weighted_supcon_loss() implements the xg-SupCon objective with w_ij = 0.1 + 0.9*r_ij, and build_pair_weights() correctly computes r_ij = 1 - q_i^T*q_j. Warmup logs show real training (decreasing loss over 4 epochs), finetune logs show 6 downstream epochs with checkpoint selection by best validation accuracy. All 18+ Waterbirds benchmark run directories contain config.yaml, warmup_log.csv, finetune_log.csv, results.json, and warmup checkpoints. Figures exist as both PDF and PNG. No evidence of fabrication or number mismatches.

Finally, clean up the released artifacts so each run has a single unambiguous warmup/finetune log; the current duplicated epoch blocks in some logs are not fatal, but they are below the standard expected for a polished NeurIPS submission.
